content.problemStatement:
Chefina has an array AAA consisting of NNN positive integers.

A permutation subsequence of length MMM is a subsequence that represents a permutation of length MMM.

Now, Chefina asks Chef to find the count of permutation subsequences in array AAA.
The count can be very large, so output it modulo 100000000710000000071000000007 (109+7)(10^9 + 7)(109+7).

As a reminder:


A subsequence of an array is obtained by deleting some (possibly zero) elements from the array without changing the order of the remaining elements.
A permutation of length MMM is an array of length MMM in which every element from 111 to MMM occurs exactly once.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the length of array AAA.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers, the elements of the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the count of permutation subsequences in array AAA, modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 200000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains an integer N, the length of the array A. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the number of permutation subsequences of A modulo 1000000007.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first array, cnt[1]=1, cnt[2]=2, cnt[3]=1, cnt[4]=1, giving products 1,2,2,2 and sum 7.

solution.approach: A subsequence is a permutation of length M iff its maximum element equals M and it contains every integer from 1 to M exactly once. Let cnt[x] be the number of occurrences of value x in the whole array. For a fixed M, we can choose any one occurrence of each value 1…M independently, giving cnt[1]·cnt[2]·…·cnt[M] different subsequences. The total answer is the sum of these products for all M for which every cnt[1..M] is non‑zero. While scanning M from 1 upwards we keep a running product prod = prod * cnt[M] (mod MOD). As soon as cnt[M] = 0 the product becomes zero and all larger M contribute nothing, so we can stop. The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case after counting frequencies.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (count frequencies and a single linear scan).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the frequency array (size max(Ai)+1).

code.Python3:
import sys
MOD = 10**9 + 7

def solve():
    data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().split()))
    it = iter(data)
    T = next(it)
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = next(it)
        freq = [0] * (N + 2)
        max_val = 0
        for _ in range(N):
            x = next(it)
            freq[x] += 1
            if x > max_val:
                max_val = x
        ans = 0
        prod = 1
        for m in range(1, max_val + 1):
            if freq[m] == 0:
                break
            prod = (prod * freq[m]) % MOD
            ans = (ans + prod) % MOD
        out_lines.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
